title: DPbD Integration with the 7 PDP Principles
description: Operationalisation of DPbD for each of the 7 PDP Principles under Act 709 (Sections 6–12). Source: JPDP DPbD Guideline (issued 30 April 2026; PCP No. 2/2025 substantive content).

dpbdElements: Data minimisation: only process if necessary; aggregate or anonymise where possible
Predetermination: purpose and legal basis established before processing
Specificity: purposes specified and explicit
Data avoidance: avoid processing personal data altogether where possible
Differentiation: legal basis and purpose differentiated per processing activity
Relevance: legal basis applied correctly and connected to specific purpose
Necessity: each data type necessary for specified purpose
Limitation: no further processing beyond original purposes; technical/organisational measures (hashing, encryption, contracts)
Review: regular reviews of necessity
Cessation: cease processing when legal basis no longer applies
Adjust: modify processing if legal basis validly changes
Allocation of responsibility: clear apportionment between joint controllers
Privacy enhancing technologies
Consent: freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous; withdrawal as easy as giving consent
Clarity: clear, plain, concise, intelligible language
Semantics: clear meaning to the audience
Accessibility: easily accessible to data subject
Contextual: provided at the relevant time and in the appropriate form
Relevance: applicable to the specific data subject
Universal design: accessible to all subjects, including machine-readable formats
Comprehensible: fair understanding for children/vulnerable groups
Multi-channel: provided across multiple channels and media
Layered: balances completeness and understanding
integrations.deceptivePatternsNote: All deceptive design patterns are prohibited under DPbD.

dpbdElements: Predetermination: legal basis of disclosure established before disclosure
Data avoidance: pseudonymised/aggregated data preferred
Differentiation: legal basis per disclosure activity
Relevance: legal basis correctly applied; data relevant to disclosure
Necessity: disclosure only if not possible to fulfil purpose otherwise
Review: regular reviews of necessity
Cessation: cease disclosure when legal basis ceases; require third party to securely destroy
Adjust: modify disclosure when legal basis validly changes
Security: technical (hashing, encryption) and organisational (policies, contracts) measures
ISMS: operative means of managing security policies and procedures
Risk analysis: threat modelling and attack surface analysis
Security by design: security requirements early in system design and development
Maintenance: regular review and testing of software, hardware, systems, services
Access control management: only authorised personnel; differentiated privileges
Access limitation (number): minimal number of people with access
Access limitation (content): only attributes needed for the operation
Access segregation: no single individual has comprehensive access to all subjects' data
Secure transfers and secure storage with risk-based isolation
Pseudonymisation as soon as direct identifiability no longer needed; identification keys stored separately
Backups/logs protected with audit trails reviewed regularly
Disaster recovery and business continuity
Protection according to risk: data presenting special risks isolated where possible
Security incident response management: detect, contain, handle, report, learn
Incident management with notification to supervisory authority and data subjects
Data minimisation: periodically consider whether data is still adequate, relevant, necessary
Deletion and anonymisation: clear internal procedures and functionalities
Effectiveness: ensure anonymised data cannot be re-identified; deleted data cannot be recovered (test this)
Automation: deletion of certain data should be automated
Retention criteria: data and length of retention defined per purpose
Justification: rationale and legal grounds disclosed
Enforcement of retention policies with internal tests
Backups/logs: retention defined for backups and logs
Data flow: limit temporary storage; minimise copies
Data source: reliable sources
Degree of accuracy: as accurate as necessary for purpose
Measurably accurate: reduce false positives/negatives, including in automated decisions and AI
Verification: verify correctness with data subject at different processing stages
Rectification: facilitate rectification without delay on request
Error propagation avoidance: mitigate accumulated error in processing chain
Access (linked): data subjects given information and access to control accuracy
Continued accuracy: tests of accuracy at critical steps
Up to date: update where necessary for purpose
Data design: predetermined choices instead of free text where helpful
Clarity: clear, plain, concise, intelligible information about how to exercise rights
Accessibility: mechanisms easily accessible
Contextual: at relevant time and appropriate form
Universal design: machine-readable formats included
Comprehensible: fair understanding of rights and limits
Multi-channel: rights mechanisms across multiple channels and media
Quick options in account profile: download, edit, delete
Tracking and contact for further support
